text: Atom trapping was utilized to create a new type of ceria-based catalysts with atomically dispersed Ru₁O₅ species located at the (100) facets confirmed by various spectroscopic studies and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. These single-atom species exhibited ruthenium activity that was distinct from conventional metal/ceria systems. They demonstrated high catalytic activity toward NO oxidation, a reaction that generally requires high loadings of expensive platinum-group metals in diesel aftertreatment systems. The resulting Ru₁/CeO₂ catalyst exhibited stability tests revealed that the single-atom Ru species had no decline in catalytic performance following repeated thermal cycling, ramping, and moisture exposure. Moreover, the catalyst exhibited enhanced NOₓ storage performance at low temperatures due to the rapid formation of stable Ru-NO species and fast NOₓ spill-over on the CeO₂ surface, and as low as 0.05 wt % Ru was sufficient for the NOₓ storage. In contrast to the volatile nature of RuO₂ nanoparticles, the Ru₁O₅ species on the ceria surface were stable throughout air/steam calcination at temperatures up to 750 °C. The combination of DFT calculations and in situ DRIFTS and mass spectroscopy revealed Ru(II) ions on the ceria surface and detailed the mechanisms of NO storage and oxidation. Importantly, the Ru₁/CeO₂ catalyst exhibited high activity toward the NO reduction by CO at low temperatures, and with Ru loading as low as 0.1–0.5 wt %. Modulation-excitation in situ infrared and XPS studies further elucidated the elementary steps of the low-temperature NO reduction over the Ru₁/CeO₂ catalyst and the roles of oxygen vacancies and Ce³⁺ sites in the activity enhancement. Overall, single-atom ruthenium catalysts were demonstrated to be a robust and cost-efficient alternative to current catalysts for exhaust treatment of diesel emissions.
